coredata.dc:title: Metal-free nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots as an artificial nanozyme for enhanced antibacterial activity
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Huiping
Wang, Qixiang
Wang, Qiwei
Dong, Wenjuan
Liu, Yang
Hu, Qin
Song, Xiuli
Shuang, Shaomin
Dong, Chuan
Gong, Xiaojuan
coredata.dc:description: Bacterial infections have become a considerable obstacle to public health due to the emergence of antibiotic resistance. Herein, we rationally developed a novel and attractive nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (N-CNDs) as efficient metal-free artificial nanozymes that synergistically and efficiently inhibit bacterial survival by inheriting the bacteriostatic ability of tea polyphenols and light-triggered oxidase-like catalytic activity. The excellent oxidase-mimicking activity exhibited by N-CNDs in the light was mediated by superoxide radicals (•O2 −) with a high maximum reaction rate (1.59 μM⋅s−1) and a low Michaelis constant (0.421 mM). Furthermore, the constructed N-CNDs-based nanozyme was demonstrated to serve as a complex biomaterial platform for efficient sterilization by light-driven oxygen molecule generation of •O2 −. Remarkably, the inherent antibacterial ability of N-CNDs can effectively inhibit both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, particularly against Gram-positive S. aureus at a minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC90) of 375 μg⋅mL−1. The inactivation rates of N-CNDs alone agsinst E. coli and S. aureus were 59.72% and 37.15%, respectively. However, the inactivation rates of E. coli and S. aureus by N-CNDs achieved by light were as high as 97.91% and 80.02%, respectively. Encouragingly, a two-way ANOVA revealed that the concentration of N-CNDs and the duration of light exposure had a synergistic effect on antibacterial behavior, which provided favorable conditions for the sustained eradication of exposed bacteria. This synergistic strategy for bacterial eradication based on a novel metal-free carbon-based nanozyme provides a powerful and feasible approach for bacterial contamination management in food safety and environmental protection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal-free
Nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots
Oxidase-mimicking activity
Enhanced antibacterial activity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Catalytic mechanism for oxidase-mimicking activity of N-CNDs
Mechanism for oxidase-mimicking activity of N-CNDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image (Inset: enlarged HRTEM image), (b) size distribution, (c) AFM image, (d) height distribution, (e) XPS survey scan, high-resolution (f) N 1s, (g) C 1s, and (h) O 1s XPS spectra, and (i) FT-IR spectrum of N-CNDs.

Light-activated oxidase-like activity of N-CNDs. (a) UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CNDs (100 μg⋅mL−1) and TMB solution (80 μM) after treatment with different conditions at pH 3.5. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CNDs in different pH solutions. (c) Effect of N-CNDs concentration on the oxidase-mimicking activity. (d) Staircase-like behavior of the catalytic capacity of N-CNDs with the successive on (red arrows) and off (green arrows) states of a specific light source. (e) Steady-state kinetics assays of N-CNDs under light irradiation (pH = 3.5). (f) Lineweaver-Burk plot of N-CNDs used to calculate enzyme kinetic parameters. Each error bar represents the standard deviation of three independent measurements. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Effect of several scavengers on the oxidation of TMB by N-CNDs under a specific light source (20 mW⋅cm−1) for 10 min in the PBS buffer solution (0.2 M, pH = 3.5). (b) ESR spectra of DMPO + N-CNDs system without and with the irradiation of a specific light source (20 mW⋅cm−1) for 15 min in methanol.

The growth curves of (a) S. aureus, (b) B. subtilis, (c) E. coli, and (d) P. vulgaris treated with different concentrations of N-CNDs (0, 75, 150, 225, 300, and 375 μg⋅mL−1). Bacterial viability of (e) S. aureus, (f) B. subtilis, (g) E. coli, and (h) P. vulgaris incubated with different concentrations of N-CNDs (0, 75, 150, 225, 300, and 375 μg⋅mL−1).

(a) Images of E. coli and S. aureus treated with the same concentrations of tea polyphenols and N-CNDs (500 μg⋅mL−1) for 12 h. (b) SEM images of S. aureus and E. coli after treatment with N-CNDs (24 μg⋅mL−1) for 3 h.

(a) Fluorescence microscope images of E. coli and S. aureus treated with N-CNDs and stained with AO and PI (scale bar: 100 μm). Evaluation of colony formation of (b and c) E. coli and (d and e) S. aureus treated with different concentrations of N-CNDs and different light times. The red boxes in (b) and (d) show the growth inhibition of bacteria after treatment with different concentrations of N-CNDs in the absence of light. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
The antibacterial mechanisms of N-CNDs under (a) normal and (b) light irradiation conditions.
Schematic illustration of the preparation process of N-CNDs and its application in bacterial inactivation.
Comparison of kinetic parameters of carbon nanodots-based nanozymes.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Cu-CDs: Cu-doped CDs; CDN/Au: N, Au co-doped carbon dots; N-CDs: nitrogen-doped carbon dots; TA-NCDs: nitrogen-doped carbon dots; N-CNDs: nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Two-way ANOVA results on the effects of N-CNDs concentration and light duration on bacterial viability. All p-values are significance (p < 0.05).

head.ce:title: Metal-free nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots as an artificial nanozyme for enhanced antibacterial activity

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bacterial infections have become a considerable obstacle to public health due to the emergence of antibiotic resistance. Herein, we rationally developed a novel and attractive nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (N-CNDs) as efficient metal-free artificial nanozymes that synergistically and efficiently inhibit bacterial survival by inheriting the bacteriostatic ability of tea polyphenols and light-triggered oxidase-like catalytic activity. The excellent oxidase-mimicking activity exhibited by N-CNDs in the light was mediated by superoxide radicals (•O2 −) with a high maximum reaction rate (1.59 μM⋅s−1) and a low Michaelis constant (0.421 mM). Furthermore, the constructed N-CNDs-based nanozyme was demonstrated to serve as a complex biomaterial platform for efficient sterilization by light-driven oxygen molecule generation of •O2 −. Remarkably, the inherent antibacterial ability of N-CNDs can effectively inhibit both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, particularly against Gram-positive S. aureus at a minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC90) of 375 μg⋅mL−1. The inactivation rates of N-CNDs alone agsinst E. coli and S. aureus were 59.72% and 37.15%, respectively. However, the inactivation rates of E. coli and S. aureus by N-CNDs achieved by light were as high as 97.91% and 80.02%, respectively. Encouragingly, a two-way ANOVA revealed that the concentration of N-CNDs and the duration of light exposure had a synergistic effect on antibacterial behavior, which provided favorable conditions for the sustained eradication of exposed bacteria. This synergistic strategy for bacterial eradication based on a novel metal-free carbon-based nanozyme provides a powerful and feasible approach for bacterial contamination management in food safety and environmental protection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial-induced infection diseases have become one of the most important threats to human health, causing more than 10 million deaths each year (Hu et al., 2021). The extracellular polymeric substance (EPS) secreted and produced by planktonic bacteria cause bacteria to adhere to each other in various ways, forming bacterial biofilms that are difficult to eradicate and can act as a barrier against host immune responses and some conventional antimicrobial agents (Sepehri and Sarrafzadeh, 2018; Zhang et al., 2022). Bacterial biofilms exhibit a diversity of phenotype changes and are resistance to many existing antibiotics, leading to the emergence of drug-resistant bacteria (Makabenta et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2022). Drug-resistant bacteria increase the difficulty of clinical anti-infection therapy, mainly due to adverse effects, uncertain outcomes, long treatment times, and high costs (Panda et al., 2022). To make matters worse, the development of new antibiotics has come to a near standstill due to the prevalence of resistant bacteria, strict regulatory procedures in various countries, and low market returns. Recent advances in nanoscience and nanotechnology have spawned many promising antimicrobial therapeutic approaches that can effectively control and treat pathogenic microorganisms (Panda et al., 2022; Wu et al., 2022). Compared with traditional antibiotics (small organic molecules), antibacterial nanomaterials have good recyclability or biodegradability, the ability to inhibit efflux pumps, and the potential for multiple antibacterial actions, which can evade existing resistance mechanisms and may not be susceptible to resistance selection (Cui et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2022). Similarly, nanotechnology, as an attractive alternative to conventional antibacterial therapies, offers a promising toolkit for enriching treatment strategies for bacterial infections (Carrillo-Carrión et al., 2019; Miller et al., 2021).

Nanozymes, i.e., nanomaterials that mimic the activity of natural enzymes, have been widely used in biosensor development, cancer therapy, environmental protection, and antibacterial therapy (Xi et al., 2022). Natural enzymes can kill bacteria by catalyzing the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in the host defense, thereby damaging the bacterial membranes and inactivating proteins. Nevertheless, natural enzymes display some drawbacks that cannot be ignored, including low stability, high cost, difficulty in storage, and harsh catalytic reaction conditions. Fortunately, nanozymes can overcome these shortcomings very well due to the unique physicochemical properties of nanomaterials (Chen et al., 2021). In addition, various strategies have been investigated to enhance the catalytic activity of nanozymes, such as adjusting the specific surface area, forming hybrids, and applying external stimuli (Lin et al., 2021). Recently, a variety of nanozymes, including metal-based nanoparticles (MNPs) (Bi et al., 2022; Liao et al., 2022), metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) (Hu et al., 2021), carbon-based nanomaterials (Xi et al., 2022), etc. have been reported and have demonstrated outstanding broad-spectrum antibacterial effects against drug-resistance strains.

Carbon-based nanozymes have attracted extensive attention in the treatment of bacterial infections due to their low toxicity and long-term safety to the environment. Carbon nanodots (CNDs), a class of carbon-based nanoparticle (< 10 nm) with sp2 carbon structure, have recently emerged as a new strategy to reversibly modulate enzymatic activities using light due to their absorption in a broad spectral region (Wang et al., 2015). The light absorption of CNDs covers a wide spectrum region from ultraviolet (UV) to near-infrared radiation, and it acts as an external stimulus driving electron transfer and generating reactive oxygen species (ROS) in many redox reactions and physical processes that exhibit significant phototoxicity (Zhao et al., 2021). At present, reports on CNDs-based nanozymes as antibacterial agents mainly focus on the design of hybrid nanozymes with enzymatic activities by combining CNDs with other nanomaterials to achieve the inhibition of bacterial growth. Chitosan grafted Fe-doped-carbon dots (CS@Fe/CDs) with good peroxidase-like activity has been successfully prepared by Pan et al. (2022), which can catalyze H2O2 to generate •OH through Fenton-like reaction, thereby cleaving eDNA to eliminate bacterial biofilms. By integrating the recognition/transduction CDs and platinum nanoparticles (PtNPs), Liang et al. (2020) designed an exquisite CDs@PtNPs (CPP) nanoflare that not only exhibited efficient in vivo antibacterial performance at acidic H2O2-rich infected sites, but also in removing methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA)-infected biofilms showed significant therapeutic efficacy. However, the synthesis process of multi-component composites is usually complicated and the yield is low. In addition, the metal composite that exert the action of the nanoenzyme may be related to the leaching behavior of the metal, making is difficult to ensure the environmental safety in practical applications. Few single and metal-free CNDs have been reported to have enzymatic activity and inhibit bacterial growth.

Tea polyphenols was obtained from Henan Zhongsheng Chemical Products Co., Ltd. Ethylenediamine, 3,3ʹ,5,5ʹ-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt hydrate (EDTA•2Na), iso-propyl alcohol (IPA), tryptophan (Try), p-benzoquinone (BQ), 5,5ʹ-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO), acridine orange (AO), propidium iodide (PI), and tert-butyl alcohol were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemical reagents were of analytical grade or above and used directly without further purification. Ultrapure water (≥18.25 MΩ⋅cm) used in the experiments was obtained from Molecular purification system (Shanghai, China).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were used to characterize the size and height of N-CNDs by a JEOL JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (Tokyo, Japan) and a Veeco Nanoscope Quadrex AFM (Bruker AFM Sensors, Camarillo, CA, U.S.A.), respectively. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were measured with an AXIS ULTRA DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Kratos, Tokyo, Japan). Elemental analysis was analyzed on a Vario EL CUBE elemental analyzer (Hanau, Germany). Fourier transform infrared spectra were performed on a Bruker Tensor II Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (FT-IR, Bremen, Germany) in the range of 4000–800 cm−1 with a resolution of 1 cm−1. The crystallographic structure of N-CNDs was investigated by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD, D8 Advance, Bremen, Germany) using diffraction angles (2θ) of 5-90°. The UV–vis absorption spectra were collected on a Lambda 950 spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, Llantrisant, U.K.) from 200 to 700 nm. Fluorescence spectra were recorded using a FP-8300 spectrofluorometer (Jasco, Japan). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of bacteria were obtained by a JEOL JSM-7900F (Tokyo, Japan). The electron spin resonance (ESR) was measured by a Bruker EMXplus electron paramagnetic resonance apparatus (Karlsruhe, Germany) at ambient temperature.

N-CNDs were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method using ethylenediamine and tea polyphenols as nitrogen and carbon sources, respectively. Briefly, 0.10 g tea polyphenols and 100 μL ethylenediamine were first dissolved in 10 mL ultrapure water and the mixture was ultrasonically treated for 10 min. The obtained solution was then transferred into a poly(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 10 h. After natural cooling to room temperature (ca. 25 °C), a dark-red suspension (crude product) was firstly obtained. The crude product was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min and then dialyzed in dialysis bag (100–500 Da) for 12 h by changing the external solution every 3 h. After the solvent was removed and further lyophilized in a vacuum freeze drier, the purified N-CNDs were dispersed in ultrapure water for further use. For comparison, CNDs were fabricated under the same conditions by using tea polyphenols as the only reaction precursor.

The oxidase-mimicking activity of N-CNDs was investigated based on the direct oxidation of TMB in the absence of H2O2. Typically, 30 μL of TMB (50 mM) and 30 μL of N-CNDs (10 mg⋅mL−1) were injected into 2.0 mL PBS buffer solution (0.02 M, pH = 3.5) to get a mixture for further experiment. Subsequently, 500 W mercury-xenon lamp loaded with 365 nm filter (20 mW⋅cm−2) as a specific light source was adapted to illuminate the mixture to trigger the redox reaction between dissolved oxygen and TMB. The effect of pH and N-CNDs concentration on the reaction system was optimized using the same procedure as described above.

The kinetics of oxidase-mimicking activity was measured by recording the absorbances of TMB oxidation products at 652 nm using a UV–vis spectrophotometer. To measure the apparent kinetic parameters, different concentrations of TMB substrate were added to an PBS buffer solution (pH = 3.5) containing N-CNDs at a concentration of 100 μg⋅mL−1. The concentration-dependent spectra of the mixtures were recorded at 652 nm after 5 min of incubation under a specific light source irradiation. The relationship between the enzymatic reaction velocity and substrate concentration was fitted with Michaelis-Menten kinetics equation (Eq. 1), which is shown as follows:
(Eq. 1)
V =
V max
×
[ S ]
K m
+
[ S ]
where V and Vmax represent the initial and maximal reaction velocity, respectively. [S] and Km are TMB concentration and Michaelis constant, respectively. V is obtained from the concentration to time of the TMB-derived oxidation product, which can be calculated from its absorbance data (peak at 652 nm) by the Lambert-Beer law, where the molar absorption coefficient is 39 000 M−1⋅cm−1 (Zhu et al., 2020).

Using the double reciprocal method, Michaelis-Menten kinetics equation (Eq. 1) can be evolved into Lineweaver-Burk equation (Eq. 2). Vmax and Km can be calculated by plotting the corresponding Lineweaver-Burk plot with the reciprocal of the substrate concentration (1/[S]) as the X-axis and the reciprocal of the reaction rate (1/V) as the Y-axis.
(Eq. 2)
1 V
=
K m
V max
×
1
[ S ]
+
1
V max

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic mechanism for oxidase-mimicking activity of N-CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: Diverse kinds of ROS scavengers, including EDTA (10 mM), IPA (10 mM), Try (10 mM), and BQ (10 mM), were injected in the typical TMB oxidation reaction system. The typical sample solution was composed of PBS buffer solution (0.02 M, pH = 3.5), N-CNDs (100 μg⋅mL−1), and TMB (80 μM). The absorption spectra of the different mixtures were recorded every 5 min under a specific light source irradiation at 25 °C. The generation of superoxide anion (•O2 −) radical was further confirmed by ESR using DMPO as a spin trap. N-CNDs were ultrasonically dispersed in methanol to obtain a solution with a concentration of 1.0 mg⋅mL−1. An equal volume of N-CNDs solution (1.0 mg⋅mL−1) was mixed with DMPO, which was placed in a quartz tube and inserted into the sample chamber. The spectra and data of the mixture solution were recorded at a specific light source irradiation for 0 min and 15 min.

The detailed experimental procedure for evaluating antibacterial activity referred the previously published work (Wang et al., 2021a). S. aureus, E. coli, P. mirabilis, and B. subtilis were selected as the model bacteria to evaluate the antibacterial activity of N-CNDs. All bacteria were first cultivated overnight on Luria Bertani (LB) liquid medium at 37 °C in a bacteriological incubator. In particular, LB contained 10.0 g of sodium chloride, 5.0 g of yeast extract powder, and 10.0 g of tryptone, and its pH was adjusted to 7.4 with a small amount of sodium hydroxide. The turbidity of the freshly obtained bacterial suspension was diluted to 0.5 McFarland standards to get working concentrations in the range of 1–2 × 108 colony forming units (CFU⋅mL−1) (Gavel et al., 2018). The minimum inhibitory concentration of N-CNDs against 50% (MIC50) and 90% (MIC90) of the bacterial population were measured using the broth microdilution method. Briefly, 10 μL bacterial suspension was dispersed in 10 mL of LB medium containing various concentrations of N-CNDs (0–335 μg⋅mL−1) to shake for 10 h, and then the optical density (OD) at 600 nm was recorded every hour. To ensure the accuracy of the experimental results, all experiments were repeated three times. Prior to experiments, all vessels and reagents were autoclaved at 121 °C for 20 min to ensure sterility.

Furthermore, the effect of the light source on the antibacterial ability of N-CNDs was evaluated by plate counting method. Pretreated dilute bacterial suspensions (approximately 3–4 × 102 CFU⋅mL−1) were treated with different concentrations of N-CNDs (0, 4.8, 9.6, 14.4, 19.2, and 24.0 μg⋅mL−1) and exposed to the light source for different times (20, 40, and 60 min) and then smeared on LB agar medium. The colony forming units (CFUs) of the bacteria were detected using a tablet colony counter after 24 h of re-cultivation.

SEM images of S. aureus and E. coli were determined according to the previously reported methods (Wang et al., 2021a). After a single colony inoculated into the fresh LB medium were continuously cultivated to logarithmic phase in a shaking incubator at 37 °C, bacteria were harvested by centrifugation and then washed twice in sterile saline solution (0.9% NaCl) to remove the medium constituents and other residual chemical macromolecules. Subsequently, the treated bacteria were incubated with or without N-CNDs (500 μg⋅mL−1) for 3 h. The bacteria were then fixed in 2.5% glutaraldehyde for 2 h after being washed three times with DPBS assisted by centrifugation. Then, these bacteria were dehydrated step by step with different concentrations of alcohol solution (25%, 50%, 70%, 85%, 95%, and 100%) and finally dispersed in tert-butyl alcohol. The mixture was left in a ventilated place until the methanol was completely evaporated to successfully obtain immobilized bacteria. Finally, the treated sample powder was stick on the conductive glue to acquire SEM images of the bacteria.

For live/dead bacteria staining, S. aureus and E. coli suspensions (1.0 mL, 108 CFU‧mL−1) were placed in cell culture dishes and divided into three groups. The bacteria in first group without any treatment was served as a control group. The bacteria in the second group were treated with N-CNDs (300 μg⋅mL−1) for 3 h. And the bacteria in the third group were treated with N-CNDs for 3 h and then illuminated by the specific light source (20 mW‧cm−2) for 1 h. The three groups of bacterial suspensions were co-stained by AO (1.0 mg‧mL−1, 10 μL) and PI (1.0 mg⋅mL−1, 10 μL) for 15 min in a dark room. Further, the suspension was washed several times with sterile DPBS buffer to remove excess dyes. Finally, with the assistance of a confocal laser scanning microscopy (LSM880, Zeiss, Germany), the live/dead cells staining images were successfully acquired.

All experiments were performed in at least triplicate, and all data are presented as mean ± standard deviation. Statistical analysis was conducted by Graphpad Prism 9.0. Two-way ANOVA was used to investigate the effects of N-CNDs concentration, light duration time, and their interactions on bacterial activities. Results with p values less than 0.05 were considered statistically significant, including ***p < 0.001, **p < 0.01, and *p < 0.05, while NS was not significant.

Metal-free N-CNDs were prepared by a classical and facile one-step hydrothermal treatment of tea polyphenols and ethylenediamine. The TEM image in Fig. 1 a depicts that the particle size of N-CNDs varies from 1.50 to 5.50 nm, conforming to a normal distribution curve with an average size of approximately 3.40 nm (Fig. 1b). A representative HRTEM image inserted in Fig. 1a demonstrates that the lattice spacing of N-CNDs is ca. 0.22 nm, which is the same as the graphene (100) plane (Gavel et al., 2018; Li et al., 2022). AFM image (Fig. 1c) of N-CNDs was further applied to illustrate the topographic morphology, confirming that N-CNDs possess homogeneous distribution with a mean height of 4.30 nm (Fig. 1d). Combining the results of TEM and AFM shows that N-CNDs basically have a sphere-like structure. As shown in Fig. S1, the XRD pattern of N-CNDs displays a broad 2θ peak at 23°, which is related to the graphite lattice spacing (002), ascribing to sp2-hybridized amorphous carbon (Kitchawengkul et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2018). These results revealed that N-CNDs have highly disordered carbon atoms and a sphere-like graphitized structure.

XPS and FT-IR analysis were performed to characterize the chemical state of the elements and the surface composition of N-CNDs. XPS survey scan (Fig. 1e) suggests that N-CNDs are mainly composed of C, N, and O elements, corresponding to C 1s (286.8 eV), N 1s (402.8 eV), and O 1s (531.8 eV), respectively (Fig. 1d). The weight percentages of C, H, N, and O measured by elemental analysis were 34.52 wt%, 6.53 wt%, 18.22 wt%, and 40.73 wt% (Table S1a), respectively, and the corresponding empirical formula for a N-CND was approximately C2H5NO2 (Table S1b). The high-resolution spectrum of N 1s shows two peaks at 399.4 and 400.8 eV, which ascribes to pyrrolic N and graphitic N, respectively (Fig. 1f) (Xi et al., 2022). The XPS spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 1g) can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.7, 285.8, and 288.0 eV, assigning for C–C/CC, C–N/C–O, and CO, respectively (Li et al., 2021; Ye et al., 2020). Moreover, the O 1s spectrum contains three peaks at 531.1, 532.2, and 535.2 eV, corresponding to CO, C–O, and O–CO, respectively (Fig. 1h) (Gong et al., 2019; Panda et al., 2022). XPS results depict that the abundant hydrophilic groups such as –NH2, –OH, and –COOH can be found on the surface of N-CNDs, ensuring excellent water dispersity of N-CNDs, which is also confirmed by the FT-IR spectroscopic results. In the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1i), N-CNDs show a characteristic broad and sharp peak at about 3100–3700 cm−1, manifesting the existence of hydroxyl and amino functional groups on its surface (Li et al., 2021). The peaks at 1577, 1491, 1336, and 1160 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of CC, –NO2, C–N, and C–O, respectively (Alaş and Genç, 2021; Li et al., 2021). These results are generally consistent with those of XPS.

The optical performance of N-CNDs was further investigated in detail. The as-prepared N-CNDs exhibit bright blue fluorescence with an emission wavelength of 463 nm under an excitation wavelength of 394 nm (Fig. S2a), while the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of CNDs correspond to 360 nm and 438 nm (Fig. S2b), respectively, indicating that the nitrogen doping led to a significant red-shift in the fluorescence spectra of N-CNDs. This red-shift may originate from the narrowing of the HOMO-LUMO gap in N-CNDs, which is mainly attributed to the presence of graphitic nitrogen structures in N-CNDs (Holá et al., 2017). Both N-CNDs (Fig. S2c) and CNDs (Fig. S2d) have similar characteristic as previously reported that the excitation wavelength-dependent behavior, i.e., the emission wavelength shifts from 430 nm to 505 nm when the excitation wavelength is changed from 280 nm to 480 nm, which may be attributed to the existence of energetically different emission traps (Tripathi et al., 2020). Furthermore, the UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CNDs and CNDs were also investigated in detail (Fig. S3). CNDs have a typical sharp absorption peak at 240 nm corresponding to the π→π* transition of the graphite sp2 domain, while N-CNDs show a broad peak at 302 nm related to the n→π* absorption band of C–N (Cui et al., 2021). Compared with CNDs, the characteristic absorption peak of N-CNDs was significantly red-shifted after doping with nitrogen atoms, which may be attributed to the electron doping effect of graphitic nitrogen that significantly changed the electronic energy levels of N-CNDs, resulting in the red-shifted absorption spectrum of N-CNDs (Sarkar et al., 2016). The fluorescence stability of N-CNDs and CNDs in different environments was studied. The photoluminescence (PL) intensity of N-CNDs and CNDs hardly decayed after continuous 365 nm UV irradiation for 180 min (Fig. S4a and Fig. S4b) or continuous xenon lamp irradiation for 5400 s (Fig. S4c and Fig. S4d). In addition, N-CNDs and CNDs maintained relatively stable PL intensity in different pH solutions (Fig. S5a and Fig. S5b) and high concentration KCl solutions (Fig. S5c and Fig. S5d). These results illustrate that N-CNDs and CNDs have different optical properties, while they both have excellent fluorescence stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for oxidase-mimicking activity of N-CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: It is great significance to study the mechanism of the oxidase-mimicking activity of N-CNDs for the continuous study of enzyme-like carbon nanodots. For the oxidase-mimicking activity of N-CNDs, some reactive oxygen species (ROS) may be involved, including superoxide anion (•O2 −), hydroxyl (•OH), oxygen vacancies, and singlet oxygen (1O2). To explore the catalytic mechanism of N-CNDs, EDTA•2Na, IPA, BQ, and Try were selected as scavengers for eliminating oxygen vacancies, •OH, •O2 −, and 1O2, respectively (Jin et al., 2020). In Fig. 3 a, the absorbance at 652 nm was almost completely inhibited only after the addition of BQ, indicating that the dissolved oxygen may react with N-CNDs to generate •O2 −, which plays a key role in the oxidase-mimicking catalytic system. Furthermore, electrochemical cyclic voltammetry was used to detect the redox potential of N-CNDs (Fig. S6), demonstrating that N-CNDs displays almost no redox peak and a redox potential. Therefore, the redox potential is not suitable for assessing whether N-CNDs can produce •O2 −. However, ESR is considered to be the most widely used and convincing method for the detection of •O2 − (Li et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2021). So we employed ESR to confirm the production of short-lived •O2 − using DMPO as the •O2 −-sensitive trapping agents. DMPO can capture •O2 − to generate DMPO-•O2 − compounds in methanol solution and exhibit a distinct ESR signal with a relative peak pattern of 1:1:1:1 in the four-line spectrum (Bi et al., 2022). As depicted in Fig. 3b, pure DMPO in N-CNDs is ESR silent (black line) in the absence of a specific light source, while the distinctive •O2 − (red line) peak can be clearly identified in the ESR spectrum in the presence of a specific light source, further demonstrating that •O2 − is the main type of free radical produced by N-CNDs. To fully illustrate the mechanism of catalytic activity of light-controlled N-CNDs on the production of •O2 −, N-CNDs and CNDs were used to investigate their catalytic effect on TMB under light irradiation. Under the same experimental conditions, CNDs did not display the same oxidase-mimicking activity as N-CNDs (Fig. S7), indicating that the enzyme-mimicking activity of N-CNDs originates from the introduction of nitrogen, which is similar to many reported studies that the entry of pyrrolic nitrogen and graphitic nitrogen into the carbon skeleton can improve the internal structure of CNDs and regulate the electron energy levels, thus enhancing the catalytic activity of N-CNDs (Tian et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021b). In conclusion, we suggest that the mechanism of N-CNDs-mediated TMB oxidation reaction is that •O2 − formed by N-CNDs catalyzing oxygen molecules under a specific light source can react with TMB to form ox-TMB.

Since tea polyphenols have been reported to inhibit bacterial growth (Wang et al., 2021c), we speculated that N-CNDs may inherit this property of tea polyphenols and play a role in the antibacterial field. Therefore, the antibacterial properties of N-CNDs were tested using Gram-positive bacteria Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and Bacillus subtilis (B. subtilis) and Gram-negative bacteria Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Proteus mirabilis (P. vulgaris) as model microorganisms. The viability of bacteria was assessed by comparing the OD600 values of the bacterial suspension after incubating the same amount of bacterial suspension with different concentrations (0, 75, 150, 225, 300, and 375 μg⋅mL−1) of N-CNDs for 10 h under the same conditions. It can be observed that the growth inhibition effect of N-CNDs on the four bacteria was gradually enhanced as its concentration increased from 0 μg⋅mL−1 to 375 μg⋅mL−1 (Fig. 4 a–d). When the concentration of N-CNDs reached 375 μg⋅mL−1, the bacterial viability of S. aureus, B. subtilis, E. coli, and P. vulgaris decreased to 9.89% (Fig. 4e), 55.83% (Fig. 4f), 58.22% (Fig. 4g), and 49.75% (Fig. 4h), respectively, corresponding to the antibacterial rates of about 90.11%, 44.17%, 41.78%, and 50.25%. These results indicated that N-CNDs had a significant antibacterial effect against S. aureus with a MIC90 value of 375 μg⋅mL−1, while they showed little antibacterial activity against P. vulgaris with a MIC50 value of 375 μg⋅mL−1. More importantly, N-CNDs still showed good antibacterial activity against S. aureus after being left at room temperature for 3 months. We can find from Table S2 that the antibacterial activity of N-CNDs is comparable to other materials that have been reported (Chai et al., 2021; Hao et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2023), which predicts that N-CNDs are an antibacterial nanomaterial with extremely promising applications.

To determine whether the antibacterial properties of N-CNDs were solely derived from the antibacterial ability of tea polyphenols, bacterial suspensions (S. aureus and E. coli) were incubated with the same concentrations of N-CNDs or tea polyphenols (500 μg⋅mL−1) for 12 h, and the number of colonies was counted by plate counting method. The results proved that tea polyphenols had a strong antibacterial activity against S. aureus (92.3%), but a weak antibacterial activity against E. coli (31.1%) (Fig. 5 a and Fig. S8). More importantly, the antibacterial effect of N-CNDs against S. aureus and E. coli were markedly stronger than that of tea polyphenols, suggesting that the process of assembling and carbonizing tea polyphenols to form N-CNDs not only perfectly retained the bacterial growth inhibitory properties of tea polyphenols themselves, but also conferred superior antibacterial ability to N-CNDs than tea polyphenols. SEM, a routine analytical method, was utilizeutilized to intuitively examine the disruption of the bacterial membranes of S. aureus and E. coli after treatment with N-CNDs. As depicted in Fig. 5b, the cell walls of S. aureus cells and E. coli cells in the control group were regular and smooth, while the cell walls of E. coli exhibited slight contraction, deformation, and collapse after 3 h of treatment with N-CNDs, and the cell walls of some S. aureus were completely ruptured and broken. In short, N-CNDs not only inherited the antibacterial properties of tea polyphenols, but also further enhanced the antibacterial effect during the carbonization process and eventually inhibited the growth of bacteria by damaging their cell walls.

In summary, we have fabricated low-cost, biocompatible, and water-dispersible metal-free N-CNDs with oxidase-mimicking activity, whose own antibacterial activity can be enhanced by light driving and exhibit good antibacterial activity. With the assistance of scavengers and ESR, we determined that the light-driven mimic oxidase activity of N-CNDs is mediated by •O2 − generated by the oxygen activation of N-CNDs. The small particle size and abundant pyrrole nitrogen active sites of N-CNDs lead to loss of cell membrane integrity and rupture of cell membrane, which can be used effectively against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. Light-driven generation of •O2 − radicals has been proved to be the active oxygen responsible for photocatalytic sterilization, which can synergize with the inherent antibacterial activity of N-CNDs to significantly improve the antibacterial effect and achieve more efficient sterilization. Taken together, N-CNDs, as a low-cost metal-free carbon-based nanozyme, are expected to be an extremely promising future material for disinfection, sterilization, environmental remediation, and other wound healing applications.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

Huiping Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Software, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Qixiang Wang: Investigation, Validation, Formal analysis. Qiwei Wang: Software, Formal analysis. Wenjuan Dong: Software, Formal analysis. Yang Liu: Resources, Formal analysis, Validation. Qin Hu: Formal analysis, Methodology. Xiuli Song: Formal analysis, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Shaomin Shuang: Project administration, Supervision. Chuan Dong: Project administration, Supervision. Xiaojuan Gong: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Supervision, Funding acquisition, All persons who have made substantial contributions to the work reported in the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Central Guidance Fund for Science and Technology Development (YDZJSX2021A004), National Natural Science Foundation of China (21705101, 22274090, and 21874087), Science and Technology Innovation Project of Higher Education in Shanxi Province (2020L0047), and the Applied Basic Research Programs of Science and Technology Commission Foundation of Shanxi Province (201901D111292).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the Supplementary data to this article.

Elemental analysis of N-CNDs; XRD pattern of N-CNDs; Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N-CNDs and CNDs; Fluorescence behavior of N-CNDs and CNDs; UV-vis absorption spectra of N-CNDs and CNDs; Effects of UV irradiation, xenon lamp irradiation, pH, and ionic strength on PL intensities of N-CNDs and CNDs; Cyclic voltammograms of blank and N-CNDs; UV-vis absorption spectra of TMB, CNDs + TMB, and N-CNDs + TMB under light irradiation; Comparisons of the MICs of N-CNDs and other metal-free CDs against S. aureus, B. subtilis, E. coli, and P. vulgaris; Evaluation of colony formation of E. coli and S. aureus treated with tea polyphenols and N-CNDs. (PDF)
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1
